grammar.usage: Completed past actions, past habits/routines, permanent past situations, general past truths, main events in a story
grammar.example: I saw Maria for the first time at Henry's party.

grammar.usage: Actions in progress at a specific past time, temporary past situations, annoying past habits, background information in a story
grammar.example: When my dad met my mum, he was working as a bus driver.

grammar.usage: Actions before a specific past time, completed actions before another past action, situations continuing up to a past moment
grammar.example: When we got to the airport, I realized I had left my passport at home.

grammar.usage: Actions continuing up to a past moment, actions in progress before another past action showing cause/effect
grammar.example: We had been running for six miles - and we still had a mile to go!

blocks.content: 1. STRUCTURE OF PAST SIMPLE & PAST CONTINUOUS

rows: Statement

S + V2/ed + O
EX: I saw Maria for the first time at Henry's party.

S + was / were + Ving + O
EX: When my dad met my mum, he was working as a bus driver.
Negative

S + didn't + Vbare + O
EX: When I went to get the tickets, I realized I didn't have any money.

S + was / were + NOT + Ving + O
EX: Richard wasn't watching TV when the phone rang.
Question

Did + S + Vbare + O
EX: Did you enjoy the film?

Was / Were + S + Ving + O
EX: Was Zoe always taking your things when you shared a room?

* Can use "did" in affirmative sentences for emphasis
EX: Why didn't you pass the test?
→ I did pass the test, I got a B. Who told you I didn't?

* When a past action interrupts another ongoing action:
→ Past simple + Past continuous
* Do not use past continuous for past habits.
* Do not use stative verbs.

blocks.content: 3. USAGE OF PAST SIMPLE & PAST CONTINUOUS

rows:
- Completed past actions
- Past habits
- Permanent past situations
- General past truths
- Main events in a story

- Actions happening at a specific moment in the past
- Temporary situations in the past
- Annoying habits (usually with 'always')
- Continuous actions over a period of time
- Background information in a story

richGrammar.title: B) PAST PERFECT SIMPLE & PAST PERFECT CONTINUOUS

blocks.content: 1. STRUCTURE OF PAST PERFECT SIMPLE & PAST PERFECT CONTINUOUS

rows: Statement

S + had + V3/ed (+ O)
EX: When we got to the airport, I realized I had left my passport at home.

S + had + been + Ving (+ O)
EX: I had been listening to a new playlist when my sister started making a funny noise.
Negative

S + hadn't + V3/ed (+ O)
EX: Jack hadn't played chess before so I showed him what to do.

S + hadn't + been + Ving (+ O)
EX: Ian hadn't been working at the factory when he was made a manager.
Question

Had + S + V3/ed (+ O)
EX: Had you ever been to America when you were at 18?

Had + S + been + Ving (+ O)
EX: Had John been getting ready for the final competition?

* Sometimes there is no difference in meaning between Past Simple & Past Perfect Simple
→ We lived / had lived next to the gym for a couple of months before I decided to join.

* When listing past actions, use Past Perfect Simple for the first action and Past Simple for subsequent ones
→ I had already had one flying lesson, which was great fun, and I knew that I wanted to be a pilot.

* Use with "It was the first/second/etc time"
→ It was the first time I'd made a speech in public.

* Actions continuing up to and in the past
→ When you saw us, we had been running for six miles - and we still had a mile to go!

* Actions that happened immediately before a past moment
→ Sarah looked tired because she had been exercising all morning.

blocks.content: 3. USAGE OF PAST PERFECT SIMPLE & PAST PERFECT CONTINUOUS

rows:
- Actions happening before another action in the past
- Actions completed before a specific past time
- Past actions with results in the past

- Actions continuing up to a past moment
- Temporary situations or series of actions in the past
- Past action in progress showing a cause/effect relationship

richGrammar.title: C) WOULD, USED TO, BE/GET USED TO

blocks.content: 1. WOULD, USED TO, BE/GET USED TO COMPARISON

rows: Structure
would + Vbare
used to + Vbare

be / get used to + Ving
be / get used to + N
Usage

Past habits
EX: When I was young, my grandfather would take me to the park to play.

Past habits / states
EX: I used to smoke before.

Actions or states that are no longer strange
EX: I didn't like being the goalkeeper at first but now I'm used to it.
Notes

Do not use in negative or question sentences; use "would never" instead.
EX: We would never play games together as a family when I was growing up.

Can be used in question and negative sentences, or replace with "never used to".
EX: He didn't use to play hockey here.
EX: He never used to play hockey here.

For past states, only use "used to", not "would".
✓ I used to have a car.
✗ I would have a car.

be used to + Ving → accustomed to (spoken English)
EX: I am used to getting up early.

get used to + Ving → process of becoming accustomed
EX: You might find it strange at first but you will soon get used to it.

be accustomed to + Ving → accustomed to (written English)
EX: I am accustomed to getting up early.

be familiar with + N/Ving → understanding a field/topic
EX: The committee chose Sharon because she is familiar with this field.

vocabulary.meaning: a game for two players, played on a chessboard with 24 round pieces
vocabulary.example: In the evening, they would often play checkers or draughts together.

vocabulary.meaning: a popular board game in which players buy, sell, and trade properties
vocabulary.example: We didn't feel like playing Scrabble because we had just finished a long game of Monopoly.

vocabulary.meaning: a board game in which players try to make words from letters
vocabulary.example: My grandmother is extremely good at Scrabble and always wins.

vocabulary.meaning: a time long ago in history
vocabulary.example: In the distant past, people used to travel by horse-drawn carriages.

vocabulary.meaning: busy; having formally agreed to marry; involved in conflict
vocabulary.example: The phone line was engaged when I tried to call him, but I later found out they had just got engaged.

vocabulary.word: have / give / throw a surprise party
vocabulary.meaning: to organize an unexpected party for someone
vocabulary.example: We decided to throw a surprise party for our teacher's birthday.

vocabulary.meaning: a long piece of music written for a large orchestra
vocabulary.example: Beethoven's Fifth Symphony is one of the most famous pieces of classical music.

vocabulary.meaning: gasping for air, especially after physical exertion
vocabulary.example: I was completely out of breath after running up five flights of stairs.

vocabulary.meaning: large flat shellfish that can be eaten raw or cooked, and sometimes produce pearls
vocabulary.example: We ordered a platter of fresh oysters at the seaside restaurant.

vocabulary.meaning: for a large amount of time
vocabulary.example: We had to wait long before the train finally arrived.

vocabulary.meaning: to demolish a building or structure
vocabulary.example: They are planning to knock down the old library and build a modern center.

vocabulary.meaning: to perform music using a traditional Scottish wind instrument
vocabulary.example: A man in a kilt was playing the bagpipes in the middle of Edinburgh.

vocabulary.meaning: to change position so that the other side is facing up; roll over in bed
vocabulary.example: I couldn't sleep last night; I kept turning over and over.

vocabulary.meaning: to lie in bed unable to sleep
vocabulary.example: She lay awake for hours, worrying about her exam the next day.

vocabulary.meaning: to be in or move into a horizontal position on a surface
vocabulary.example: The cat decided to lie on the sofa all afternoon.

vocabulary.meaning: to put something down carefully in a flat position
vocabulary.example: She laid the tablecloth on the table before serving dinner.

vocabulary.meaning: to say something that is not true in order to deceive someone
vocabulary.example: He lied about his age to get into the club.

vocabulary.meaning: a team game played in a swimming pool, where players try to throw a ball into the opponent's goal
vocabulary.example: Water polo is a physically demanding sport that requires excellent swimming skills.
